globalNews.riskLevel: High but still event-driven – dominated by Iran conflict and energy pricing.
globalNews.marketImpactHorizon: Days to months, depending on whether talks with Iran succeed by the extended early-April deadline.

themes.theme: Iran war, Strait of Hormuz risk and elevated oil prices
themes.status: Active conflict with ongoing but fragile negotiations; US has extended the deadline for potential strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure by 10 days to early April.
themes.marketImpact: Oil surged from around 70 to well above 100–110 dollars and remains volatile; shipping through Hormuz faces disruption risk, and analysts warn that oil could spike towards 200 dollars if war drags into the summer.
themes.indiaImpact: India’s import bill and current account deficit worsen when oil stays above 100 dollars; rupee weakens, inflation risks rise, fiscal room shrinks, and margin pressure appears across energy-intensive sectors.
affectedAssets: Aviation, paints, chemicals and logistics stocks
themes.signalStrength: Strong – this is clearly impacting oil, FX, bond yields and equity risk premia right now.
themes.whatToTrackNext: Watch Brent’s ability to stay above or fall back below the 100–105 dollar band, any concrete ceasefire or shipping corridor agreements, and updated guidance from Indian OMCs and airlines on margins and pricing.

themes.theme: US equity correction and higher bond yields
themes.status: Ongoing – Nasdaq is in correction territory and S&P 500 is heading for a five-week losing streak as oil and yields rise.
themes.marketImpact: Higher-for-longer US rates plus war-driven inflation fears have pushed 10-year yields above 4.4 percent and triggered broad de-rating in growth stocks.
themes.indiaImpact: Historically, rising US yields with a strong dollar coincides with FII outflows from India; this pattern is visible again with heavy FII selling and pressure on Indian banks and financials.
affectedAssets: EM and India equity risk premia
themes.signalStrength: Strong – macro and positioning data both confirm it.
themes.whatToTrackNext: Monitor Fed communication, US inflation prints and labour data for any hint of rate-cut timelines; watch whether US yields stabilise or push decisively above 4.5 percent.

globalNews.summary: Geopolitics is currently dominated by the US–Iran conflict around the Strait of Hormuz, which has kept oil elevated and global risk sentiment fragile despite temporary pauses announced by the US president. Markets are increasingly discounting verbal reassurances and are reacting more to hard data like oil, shipping flows and troop movements; until there is a verifiable de-escalation, a risk premium will persist in commodities and volatility.
globalNews.view: For Indian investors, this is a time to respect macro risk, avoid overexposure to fuel-sensitive themes, and be ready to add to quality when the geopolitical fog clears rather than trying to front-run every headline.

indianMarket.summary: Broad-based risk-off selloff after a brief relief rally.
highlights: All Nifty sectoral indices closed in the red, with PSU banks, financial services, realty and autos among the worst hit, while IT and pharma fell less than the index and CPSE-related stocks showed relative resilience.

The selling was not confined to a single theme – it was a de-risking across cyclicals, rate-sensitives and prior high-flyers, with money rotating temporarily into high-cashflow defensives like oil and select IT names.

Treat today as a strong reminder that breadth and volatility matter – keep a quality bias, be cautious on leveraged or story-driven names, and prioritise balance sheets, cash flows and governance over momentum.
Gainer: Coal India (flat to slightly positive intraday, showing resilience) — Coal mining / Energy
Loser: Reliance Industries (sharp loss, among the top Nifty draggers) — Energy / Diversified

indiaNews.importanceLevel: High – energy policy and defence procurement changes are directly relevant to multiple sectors.

themes.theme: Centre cuts excise duty on petrol and diesel by ₹10 per litre but keeps pump prices unchanged
themes.status: Announced and effective from late March 2026.
themes.marketImpact: Reduces under-recoveries for Indian Oil, BPCL and HPCL, eases immediate balance-sheet stress and delays the need for sharp retail price hikes, but shifts some burden to government finances.
themes.timeHorizon: 3–12 months – will play out through FY26–27 via margins, inflation prints and fiscal math.
themes.whatToTrackNext: Monitor Q4 and Q1 margin commentary from OMCs, further changes in crude, and any follow-up moves on fuel subsidies or price hikes once war risk subsides.

themes.theme: Draft Defence Acquisition Procedure (DAP) 2026 pushes higher indigenous content and faster procurement
themes.status: Draft released; stakeholder comments invited before finalisation.
themes.marketImpact: Raises minimum indigenous content in key ‘Buy Indian-IDDM’ category from 50 percent to 60 percent and streamlines categories and timelines, structurally favouring domestic defence OEMs and component suppliers.
affectedStocks: Mid and smallcap defence suppliers (for example, Avantel, RRP Defence, Axiscades, Apollo Micro Systems).
themes.timeHorizon: 1–5 years – structural policy shift rather than a short-term event.
themes.whatToTrackNext: Final DAP 2026 text, early RFPs under the new regime, and order flows into niche domestic vendors in radars, EW, unmanned systems and aerospace.

themes.theme: Extension and liberalisation of the Textile PLI scheme application window
themes.status: Application window extended to 31 March 2026 with relaxed investment and turnover criteria.
themes.marketImpact: Encourages mid-sized and MSME textile players to commit capacity expansion by lowering thresholds, with cumulative investment proposals already exceeding ₹10,700 crore and significant job creation.
affectedStocks: Select listed textile and technical textile companies participating in PLI (names depend on final beneficiary list).
themes.timeHorizon: 2–5 years – capacity building and export growth story.
themes.whatToTrackNext: Track actual capex execution, export order trends under new FTAs and whether PLI incentives translate into sustained ROCE improvement for participating firms.

indiaNews.summary: India has moved to shield domestic fuel consumers and OMCs via a sharp excise cut, while simultaneously pushing structural policies like DAP 2026 and extended PLI schemes in textiles and food processing. Near-term policy is defensive (containing inflation and political pain from high fuel), whereas medium-term policy remains pro-manufacturing and pro-defence localisation; both directions can co-exist but have different winners and risks.
indiaNews.view: As an equity investor, it makes sense to stay overweight on beneficiaries of structural policy support (defence, textiles, railways, food processing) and treat excise and fuel interventions more as risk-management levers than long-term profit drivers.

marketRegime.description: Indices corrected about 2–2.5 percent, India VIX spiked near its 52-week high around 26.8, USDINR hit record-weak zones near 94.6 and FIIs have been large net sellers for weeks. We are in a risk-off phase driven by external shocks (oil, war, global yields) layered on top of rich domestic valuations and prior crowded trades in PSU, defence and smallcaps. Base case: a corrective, volatile but not yet panic regime – expect whipsaws, sharp up days inside a downward or sideways drift until either oil cools materially or there is clearer geopolitical de-escalation. Sizing: As of today, it is prudent to keep overall equity allocation within your strategic range but on the lower side of risk, size new positions smaller than usual, and demand cleaner technical structures and fundamentals before committing bigger capital.

signals.label: What Works
signals.value: High-quality largecaps with strong balance sheets and pricing power, particularly in energy, select IT, diversified FMCG and utilities.

signals.label: What Works
signals.value: Stocks showing clear relative strength versus Nifty (for example ONGC, certain CPSE and select defensives) where dips towards support zones may offer staggered entries.

signals.label: What Works
signals.value: Disciplined SIPs into diversified mutual funds, which naturally average into volatility.

signals.label: What Fails
signals.value: Leveraged or governance-questionable smallcaps and microcaps where liquidity can vanish quickly.

signals.label: What Fails
signals.value: Late entries into crowded themes like PSU banks, defence or railways after large prior rallies, especially when valuations are stretched.

signals.label: What Fails
signals.value: Aggressive leveraged index or options trades without strict risk controls in a high-VIX environment.

sectorRotation.summary: Sectorally, the damage is heaviest in PSU banks, financial services, autos and consumption proxies, while IT, pharma and some energy names are proving relatively resilient. Rotation is less about chasing new winners and more about fleeing crowded high-beta trades into cashflow-heavy defensives and select exporters.

sectors.name: Financials / Banks
sectors.note: Weak – Bank Nifty down about 2.7 percent; PSU banks down nearly 3.9 percent. | Treat this as a time to be selective within banks, not to blanket-buy the dip, particularly given macro and governance headwinds.
sectors.status: Underweight trading positions; for investing, focus only on top-tier names at reasonable valuations and avoid those with governance overhangs.

sectors.name: PSU Banks
sectors.note: Among the worst hit with index down around 3.9 percent. | Positioning was crowded here; the unwind can be sharp in a high-VIX, oil-shock environment.
sectors.status: Take profits into strength, avoid leverage, and do not chase; wait for deeper corrections and clearer macro calm before re-risking.

sectors.name: IT
sectors.note: Relatively resilient with Nifty IT falling only about 0.4–0.5 percent. | IT can act as a stabiliser in portfolios during this macro regime; focus on quality, not small speculative IT midcaps.
sectors.status: Gradual accumulation on dips in top-tier names for 3–5 year horizons.

sectors.name: Pharma and Healthcare
sectors.note: Mild decline of around 0.5 percent, outperforming the index. | Pharma offers a hedge against both domestic slowdown and external shocks, but stock selection and valuations still matter.
sectors.status: Reasonable to maintain or modestly increase exposure via quality leaders and diversified funds.

sectors.name: Autos
sectors.note: Down about 2.8 percent, worse than Nifty. | Higher fuel prices, global tariff noise and rich valuations argue for patience rather than bottom-fishing here.
sectors.status: Avoid aggressive new positions; existing core holdings can be retained with a long-term view but avoid adding on purely price weakness.

sectors.name: Metals
sectors.note: Nifty Metal down about 2.4 percent. | Metals remain highly tied to global growth and China sentiment; current macro mix is not supportive for fresh heavy exposure.
sectors.status: Only for tactical traders and diversified exposure; avoid concentrated bets.

sectors.name: Capital Goods / Infra / Railways
sectors.note: Weak on the day in line with broader market, but long-term policy tailwinds remain from record capex and new high-speed rail corridors. | Budget 2026 continues to favour rail and infra, but valuations in several PSUs and midcaps already discount a long runway of growth.
sectors.status: Hold structural winners but avoid chasing; accumulate gradually on deeper corrections aligned with order-book visibility.

sectors.name: Energy / Oil & Gas / Power
sectors.note: Energy index down modestly, but ONGC and some power/coal names showed relative strength and even gains. | This is one of the few pockets where fundamentals and macro (high oil, policy support) line up, but investors must factor in policy risk.
sectors.status: Constructive on integrated and upstream players with strong cash flows; cautious on pure downstream unless policy clarity improves.

breadth.summary: Internal market health is weak with elevated volatility, poor breadth and persistent foreign selling; this is not yet a ‘buy everything’ dip, but a phase to respect risk and focus on quality and position sizing.

indicators.label: Advance/Decline
indicators.value: Very weak – roughly 765 advances vs 3,420 declines on NSE (about 1:4.5), indicating broad internal weakness beneath the indices.

indicators.label: Sector Breadth
indicators.value: Almost all sectoral indices closed negative; PSU banks, defence-related indices, autos, chemicals and financials led the fall, with CPSE and some energy names the only pockets of relative strength.

indicators.label: Defensives Leading?
indicators.value: Yes, on a relative basis – IT, pharma and select FMCG/telecom names fell less or even ended flat to mildly up.

indicators.label: Cyclicals Weak?
indicators.value: Cyclicals such as banks, autos, realty, capital goods and consumption underperformed, reflecting investors de-risking from growth and leverage exposure.

indicators.label: Buying Type
indicators.value: Limited institutional and HNI buying in high-quality defensives and exporters; most of the day saw systematic and FII-led selling, especially in index heavyweights and high-beta names.

swingSetups.entry: ₹270–276, near prior consolidation and 20-day region, after today’s strength to about ₹283.
swingSetups.stopLoss: Below ₹262 on a closing basis (roughly 3–5 percent risk from mid-zone).
swingSetups.timeframe: 1–4 weeks, assuming oil stays structurally elevated but without extreme further spikes.
swingSetups.thesis: Stock broke out relative to Nifty on a broad red day with strong close and volume, indicating institutional interest; prior swing highs around 270–275 now act as potential support. High global oil prices and government support to OMCs indirectly support upstream economics; ONGC remains a cash-generative, high-dividend PSU with leverage to elevated crude. Use staggered entries and be prepared for volatility; do not add if price gaps up significantly beyond the suggested zone. R:R Approximately 1:2 if executed near the middle of the entry zone. Invalidation: A decisive close back below ₹260 or a sharp reversal in crude below the 90–95 dollar band would invalidate the thesis. Sizing: Small to medium; suitable as a satellite trade or incremental add, not a concentrated bet, given macro and policy risk.

swingSetups.entry: Around 22,650–22,700 if tested and held intraday; avoid chasing large gap-ups.
swingSetups.stopLoss: Sustained break below 22,600 on closing basis; aggressive traders can use intraday levels slightly tighter.
swingSetups.timeframe: A few sessions to two weeks, depending on news flow.
swingSetups.thesis: Analysts highlight 22,650–22,600 as near-term support; Nifty has declined sharply in a short span and may attempt a mean-reversion bounce if global cues stabilise even slightly. Any sign of easing in Iran tensions, cooling in crude towards sub-100 levels or moderation in US yields could spark short-covering. Treat this as a bounce trade, not a bottom-fishing attempt; exit quickly if price action or macro headlines deteriorate. R:R Can offer roughly 1:2 if entered near support with disciplined stop placement. Invalidation: A clean close below 22,600 accompanied by VIX spiking well above 27–28 would argue for a deeper correction towards 22,400–22,200 instead of a bounce. Sizing: Small and tactical; better suited for experienced traders who can monitor intraday rather than for casual participants.

avoidList.symbol: Highly illiquid microcaps and recent story-driven favourites
avoidList.reason: In a high-VIX, risk-off regime, these names can see air-pockets in liquidity and extreme gaps; better to avoid swing trading them until volatility normalises.

avoidList.symbol: Names with fresh governance or auditor red flags (for example, smallcaps where statutory or secretarial auditors have recently resigned without strong explanations).
avoidList.reason: Resignations of auditors in small companies like AVI Products India and SVA India increase the risk of negative surprises; they are unsuitable for leveraged or short-term trades until clarity emerges.

emergingOpportunities.overallTone: Constructive but selective – there are genuine early-stage opportunities in defence, telecom infrastructure, speciality chemicals and textiles, but they should be tracked patiently rather than chased.

ideas.theme: Niche defence and satellite communications supplier riding DAP 2026 and domestic defence capex.
ideas.triggerType: Order wins and defence policy tailwinds
earlySignals: Large incremental orders in core satcom verticals.
Growing relevance in indigenous defence electronics and radar systems.
Strong demand visibility from defence and strategic customers.
ideas.businessQualityView: Niche B2G/B2B defence electronics player with sticky relationships and high switching costs, but with customer concentration and execution risks typical for smallcaps.
ideas.valuationNote: After a sharp multibagger move in prior years, the stock has corrected about 25 percent from its 52-week high, partially digesting growth but still requiring discipline on entry price.
ideas.watchlistAction: Track for accumulation only on deep corrections and when order execution and margins remain on track; avoid chasing event-day spikes.
ideas.reasonForTracking: Recently bagged a roughly ₹460 crore multi-year contract for satellite communication equipment and AMC from Zetwerk, plus an earlier ₹122 crore order from NewSpace India, expanding its order book meaningfully relative to its size.
ideas.whatToTrackNext: Execution of the Zetwerk and NewSpace orders, working capital cycle, and any participation in new DAP 2026-driven tenders.

ideas.theme: Telecom and defence optics with capex into preform manufacturing and defence/aviation expansion.
earlySignals: Large planned capex in optical preforms, which can improve margins and reduce import dependence.
Increased focus on defence and aeronautics via HFCL Advance.
Supportive policy for telecom infrastructure and defence electronics.
ideas.businessQualityView: Established but cyclical player; moving up the value chain is positive if execution and balance sheet discipline remain strong.
ideas.valuationNote: Stock has been volatile with prior re-rating; fresh investments need to translate into ROCE improvements to justify sustained higher multiples.
ideas.watchlistAction: Monitor quarterly results, capex progress and leverage before scaling exposure; prefer staggered entries after corrections rather than momentum entry.
ideas.reasonForTracking: Approved a preferential issue of warrants worth up to ₹555 crore and plans to invest around ₹580 crore in a preform manufacturing facility plus ₹175 crore in expanding defence and aeronautics operations, signalling a push into higher value-add and supply-chain integration.
ideas.whatToTrackNext: Timelines and cost control on the preform plant, defence order inflows and export opportunities.

ideas.theme: Defence electronics and unmanned systems collaboration with a PSU major.
ideas.conviction: Low to medium (early-stage; track, do not rush)
ideas.convictionKey: low to medium (early-stage; track, do not rush)
earlySignals: MoU with a leading PSU defence electronics player.
Alignment with DAP 2026 emphasis on indigenous high-tech content.
ideas.businessQualityView: Early-stage, partnership-led business; success will depend on converting MoUs into executable orders and building scale while maintaining governance standards.
ideas.valuationNote: As with many defence smallcaps, valuations can disconnect from fundamentals in bull phases; a clear focus on order conversion and profitability is vital before building big positions.
ideas.watchlistAction: Keep on radar, but treat it as a high-risk satellite idea; prioritise governance and execution checks before any serious allocation.
ideas.reasonForTracking: Signed an MoU with Bharat Electronics to collaborate in semiconductors, unmanned systems and electro‑optics, which can open doors to larger, stickier defence projects if executed well.
ideas.whatToTrackNext: Any concrete order announcements under the MoU, margin trajectory and disclosures around R&D and intellectual property.

ideas.theme: Speciality chemicals scaling up with new capacities and strong earnings momentum.
earlySignals: New manufacturing site commissioned and participation in international investor conferences.
Management communication that core plants and R&D remain unaffected by the warehouse fire, with adequate insurance coverage.
ideas.businessQualityView: High-mix, high-value speciality chemical franchise with diversified end-markets; execution and capital allocation remain key variables.
ideas.valuationNote: Valuation is not cheap, reflecting growth expectations; corrections like the current macro-driven one can offer better entry zones if earnings momentum sustains.
ideas.watchlistAction: Look to accumulate gradually on further market-wide corrections, with position sizing commensurate with cyclical and ESG risks.
ideas.reasonForTracking: Q3FY26 results showed net profit growth of about 49 percent with ongoing capacity additions, and a recent fire incident at an external warehouse has had no impact on core operations, highlighting operational resilience.
ideas.whatToTrackNext: Capacity ramp-up, client concentration, export growth and any regulatory or ESG developments in chemical parks.

emergingOpportunities.summary: Several underfollowed Indian companies in defence electronics, telecom infrastructure and speciality chemicals have recently announced sizeable orders or capacity expansions, supported by policy tailwinds like DAP 2026 and PLI schemes. These are credible early-stage structural stories, but they sit in volatile parts of the market that are currently correcting and can overshoot both on the upside and downside. Treat these as watchlist candidates to accumulate over time, not as short-term bets; prioritise governance, balance sheets and evidence of execution over narratives and social media buzz.
emergingOpportunities.view: Treat these as watchlist candidates to accumulate over time, not as short-term bets; prioritise governance, balance sheets and evidence of execution over narratives and social media buzz.

riskWatch.overallTone: Elevated – governance, environmental and safety-related signals are flashing in a few high-profile and smaller names; risk-reward is asymmetric on the downside if ignored.

cases.issue: Abrupt resignation of part-time chairman citing practices inconsistent with his ethics, followed by a significant stock price fall and SEBI initiating a preliminary review into governance and disclosure practices.
cases.severity: High (systemically important bank and Nifty heavyweight)
cases.severityKey: high (systemically important bank and nifty heavyweight)
cases.nearTermMarketRisk: Continued valuation de-rating, higher risk premium and overhang on the broader banking index until clarity emerges, despite RBI indicating no material concerns so far.
possibleImpactAreas: Banking sector valuations and index weights
Investor perception of Indian corporate governance
cases.stance: Cautious – avoid aggressive fresh buying until investigations conclude and market has re-priced the risk; existing long-term holdings can be sized appropriately within a diversified portfolio.
cases.watchlistAction: Monitor closely but do not treat short-term price weakness alone as a buying opportunity; wait for clarity and stabilisation.
monitoringSignals: Outcomes of SEBI’s review and internal investigations.
Any further senior-level exits or adverse audit findings.
Commentary from RBI and rating agencies.
cases.whatToTrackNext: Formal findings from SEBI and the bank’s appointed law firms, board refresh, and any structural changes in risk and compliance functions.

cases.issue: Reported major sulphur trioxide leak at a plant in Maharashtra on 2 March 2026, leading to mass evacuations and raising concerns about safety practices and potential regulatory action.
cases.severity: Medium to high (for the company; limited systemic impact)
cases.severityKey: medium to high (for the company; limited systemic impact)
cases.nearTermMarketRisk: Potential for regulatory penalties, remediation costs, temporary disruption and ESG-driven de-rating if not handled transparently and decisively.
possibleImpactAreas: Financing costs and insurance premiums for similar plants
cases.stance: Avoid fresh exposure until detailed clarity on impact, liability and remediation is available.
cases.watchlistAction: High caution; treat as a red-flag case until proven otherwise.
monitoringSignals: Official disclosures from the company and regulators on the root cause and remedial measures.
Local community and media reports on health and environmental impact.
Any plant shutdowns or capacity curtailments.
cases.whatToTrackNext: Regulatory orders, company’s ESG disclosures, and whether any long-term operational restrictions are imposed.

cases.issue: Statutory auditor M/s NK Jalan & Co. withdrew consent to continue, shortly after reappointment, citing better professional engagements; company has to find a new auditor and withdrew an EGM resolution.
cases.nearTermMarketRisk: Typically, abrupt auditor changes raise questions about accounting quality or commercial terms; even if benign, they warrant caution and can lead to valuation compression.
possibleImpactAreas: Smallcap investor sentiment in similar governance-light names
Valuation multiples for companies with repeated auditor churn
cases.stance: Avoid until at least one clean audit cycle under a new auditor is completed.
cases.watchlistAction: No fresh buys; existing positions, if any, should be reviewed strictly versus alternatives.
monitoringSignals: Appointment and profile of the new auditor.
Any qualifications or emphasis-of-matter in upcoming financials.
Regulatory filings and timeliness of disclosures.
cases.whatToTrackNext: Nature and transparency of communication with exchanges and any clarifications from both outgoing and incoming auditors.

cases.issue: Social media reports highlight worker death and allegations of poor safety practices and attempted suppression of information at an Adani power plant, adding to prior ESG controversies.
cases.nearTermMarketRisk: Potential for heightened scrutiny, labour unrest and ESG-driven selling pressure; however, the financial impact from a single incident may be limited unless it triggers broader regulatory action.
possibleImpactAreas: Perceived ESG risk across the broader group
cases.stance: Cautious; treat ESG and governance risks as an explicit part of the investment case and demand a higher margin of safety.
cases.watchlistAction: If exposed, size positions conservatively and diversify; avoid fresh concentrated bets purely on valuation arguments.
monitoringSignals: Official company and regulator statements on the incident.
Follow-up media investigations and worker actions.
Any legal or compensation proceedings and policy changes.
cases.whatToTrackNext: Any pattern of repeated incidents, regulatory fines or legal judgments that may point to systemic risk.

riskWatch.summary: Recent weeks have seen a cluster of governance, auditor and ESG-related headlines, from HDFC Bank’s chairman resignation and SEBI review to chemical leaks and auditor exits at smaller firms. This underscores that, even in a strong macro and policy environment, micro-level governance and ESG risks can be major stock-specific drawdowns. In your portfolio, explicitly price in governance quality, auditor stability and ESG track record; it is better to accept slightly lower ‘story upside’ in exchange for materially lower tail-risk.
riskWatch.view: In your portfolio, explicitly price in governance quality, auditor stability and ESG track record; it is better to accept slightly lower ‘story upside’ in exchange for materially lower tail-risk.

support: Immediate: 22,650–22,600; below that, 22,400 and 22,200 as next key zones.
resistance: Upside hurdles around 23,050 first, then 23,150–23,200; above that, the earlier 23,300–23,500 supply zone.
Near-term ceiling around 52,800–53,200, where heavy call open interest is visible.

vix.pivot: India VIX near 26.8; sustained levels above 27–28 signal high stress, while a drift back below 20 would indicate improving conditions.

brent.pivot: Brent currently around 105–106; sustained moves below 95 would ease macro strain, while spikes above 115–120 would increase the risk of a deeper equity correction.

usdInr.pivot: USDINR around 94.6; a firm break above 95 would raise FX and inflation concerns, while a move back towards 92–93 would be a relief signal.

support: Credible signs of de-escalation in the Iran conflict, leading to Brent easing below the 95–100 dollar band and VIX moving back under 20.
US 10-year yields stabilising or drifting below about 4.2 percent, reducing global discount-rate pressure.
A visible slowdown or reversal in FII selling, with one or more days of strong net FII buying alongside stable USDINR.
Domestic earnings commentary that confirms demand resilience despite higher fuel and FX costs.
resistance: Brent crude breaking above 115–120 dollars and staying elevated, significantly worsening India’s oil and fiscal math.
USDINR spiking decisively beyond 95 with limited RBI ability to lean against the move, prompting further FII risk-off.
Nifty closing below 22,200 with India VIX above 28–30 and breadth deteriorating further, signalling a move from controlled correction towards panic.
Escalation in governance or regulatory issues at major financial institutions or large conglomerates, undermining confidence in key index pillars.

investorPlan.summary: Indian equities corrected sharply today, with weak breadth, high volatility and continued foreign selling, even as domestic policy remains supportive for manufacturing, defence and infra. This is not a structural end to India’s story but a sharp repricing of risk and valuations after a long bull run and external macro shocks. As a direct stock investor, your edge now lies in patience, upgrading quality, and using volatility to slowly build positions in durable franchises, while staying sceptical of crowded themes and governance-light names.

actions.action: Core largecap compounders (private banks, top IT, consumer staples, leading industrials): Hold and consider very gradual adds on deep corrections, prioritising balance-sheet strength and governance.. Despite near-term pain, these remain the backbone of an India portfolio; stagger buys over 3–6 months instead of bunching purchases into one volatile week.

Cyclicals and rate-sensitives (banks, NBFCs, autos, real estate): Neutral to underweight tactically, especially where valuations were stretched or governance questions have emerged.. Use this phase to upgrade quality rather than to chase beta; for example, be cautious around names facing SEBI probes or with heavy reliance on cheap liquidity.

PSU and policy beneficiaries (defence, railways, OMCs, select CPSEs): Long-term constructive but tactically cautious; avoid chasing high-flyers, prefer staggered entries on deeper corrections and focus on order-book visibility and execution.. Recent policy like DAP 2026, rail capex and fuel excise cuts support the medium-term story, but many PSU names had become crowded and are now correcting; treat this as a re-basing period.

Small and midcap growth / thematic plays: Selective and conservative; prioritise balance sheets, cash flows and promoter quality over narratives.. Q3FY26 data shows improving revenue breadth but still uneven profit recovery in smallcaps, so avoid over-concentration and be ready for higher volatility.

mutualFundView.summary: Continue SIPs in diversified equity funds (flexicap, large & mid, ELSS) without interruption; volatility actually improves long-term rupee-cost averaging. Avoid full lump-sum deployment on a single day; split fresh equity money into 3–6 tranches over the next 1–3 months, with more weight on days when indices retest or break key supports on manageable news rather than crisis headlines. Dry powder: Maintain some dry powder (for example 20–30 percent of the equity allocation you intend to add) to exploit a deeper correction if oil or geopolitics worsen further; do not feel compelled to be fully invested at all times.

recommendations.action: Overweight versus mid/small for fresh allocations in this regime.. These funds naturally tilt to quality and can better manage liquidity and volatility; they benefit from buying bluechips like banks, IT and energy on corrections.

Neutral to underweight new money; keep SIPs small and avoid big lump-sum entries until breadth improves and valuations cool more visibly.. Q3FY26 shows improving revenue but uneven profit recovery in smallcaps, and indices have already corrected but not fully de-risked crowded pockets.

recommendations.category: Sector/thematic funds (PSU, defence, infra, consumption, new-age)
recommendations.action: Use caution; avoid fresh aggressive buying in concentrated thematic funds after a big run and into macro uncertainty.. Policy is supportive for defence and infra, but many relevant stocks have already re-rated strongly; theme funds amplify both upside and downside, which is not ideal in a VIX-above-25 world.

recommendations.category: Hybrid / asset allocation and short-duration debt funds
recommendations.action: Reasonable parking ground for conservative money and near-term goals.. Elevated US and domestic yields suggest it is sensible to have some fixed-income ballast while you wait for equity volatility to settle.

dos: Continue SIPs and long-term asset allocation rather than reacting emotionally to a 1–3 day correction.
Prioritise quality – strong balance sheets, cash flows, governance and policy tailwinds – over momentum or social media narratives.
Use volatility to gradually accumulate preferred largecaps and structural winners at better prices, but only with clear sizing and time horizons.
Tighten risk management for trading: smaller sizes, wider but logical stops, and a focus on liquid instruments.
donts: Do not average down blindly in high-beta smallcaps or crowded PSUs just because they have fallen 20–30 percent from highs.
Do not chase intraday rebounds in illiquid names in a VIX-above-25 environment; liquidity can vanish quickly.
Do not ignore fresh governance, auditor or ESG red flags in the hope that price will recover on ‘sentiment’.
Do not over-leverage via futures or options on the assumption that this correction must reverse immediately.

news.title: Investor Bottom Line
news.summary: For long-term equity investing, this is a risk-off correction triggered by external shocks, not yet a structural end to the India story; keep SIPs running, upgrade portfolio quality, and use volatility to gradually buy strong businesses at better prices instead of trying to nail the exact bottom.

news.title: Swing Trader Bottom Line
news.summary: Treat this as a high-volatility, capital-protection phase – focus only on the cleanest, most liquid setups (like ONGC-type relative strength or clearly defined index bounces), size trades smaller, and be ready to step aside quickly if key supports break or macros worsen further.

news.title: MF Investor Bottom Line
news.summary: Stick to your plan: continue SIPs, tilt incremental lump-sum money slightly towards largecap and hybrid funds, go slow on fresh mid/smallcap or thematic exposure, and let domestic flows and asset allocation work in your favour over the next 6–12 months.
